title: 2026 02 18 FEC
displayTitle: 2026-02-18 - Finance and Expenditure Committee - Briefing on the response to the inquiry into banking competition
topic: Briefing on the response to the inquiry into banking competition
description: Wednesday 18 February 2026| Finance and Expenditure Committee Briefing on the response to the inquiry into banking competition ASB Vittoria Shortt, CEO and Managing Director ANZ New Zealand Scott St John, Chair Antonia Watson, CEO Commerce Commission Dr John Small, Chair
descriptionSummary: Briefing on the response to the inquiry into banking competition. ASB. Vittoria Shortt, CEO and Managing Director

chunks.text: And the main banks are on our banking inquiry. Six months on. So, welcome, uh, thank you for your, uh, written submission. We look forward to hopefully some introductory comments, and then we'll throw it out, open to questions. So nice for you to be here. Thank you. So, uh, and thanks for the invitation to speak again. Um, pleased to be joined today by my colleague, Adam Boyd.

Adam runs our personal bank, uh, and a lot of the products and services we've been talking about. Um, Adam looks after, um, uh, just a couple of reflections actually before we start. I think the committee's report and the recommendations have been really helpful, uh, and it provided an important spotlight on the sector.

I think while the individual recommendations are not silver bullets, when you take them in aggregate and you allow them time to embed, they definitely provide a way forward to a more competitive market. I think, uh, a couple of, um, highlights would be the changes for capital. Uh, so the reduced capital requirements for small banks will increase their ability to compete for large banks, the different capital stack.

Some components come at a lower cost December. The record switching that we saw, uh, in December, I think is really putting to bed both the perception and the reality of customers being able to move around and shopping for a good deal. And I genuinely think we are making better progress on supporting iwi, hapu small businesses with access, uh, to capital.

So there's more work to be done. We realize this and we are looking forward to working with government, um, our regulators and industry partners. I do think there's a real opportunity to take a step back at this juncture also, and to really reflect on how all of these component parts come together, how they aggregate with all of the regulatory work already underway to make sure that we're striking the right balance between safety and competition and between lowest cost and innovation.

And I think there's a really good opportunity for kofa, uh, in that regard and with those reflections. Okay. Open to questions. Okay. Who's up? The first question. Okay. Okay, we'll go, we'll go. Deputy chair on Hamilton. Thanks chair. And thanks for your, um, presentation. Um, just wanna explore further disclosing profitability on transactions on call and savings account.

And you've given obviously an example of other jurisdiction, and, and I think it's a fair point that it's possibly hard to, hard to measure given there's so much variability across your volume, size, et cetera. But is that also a little bit of a, um, a duck for cover? Like, you know, I mean, obviously to me, if I was being cynical that I'd say, well, there's still obviously a profitability element that's embedded in there.

Um, how might we get a more granular understanding of what's going on there? Yeah, great. Uh, so the FMA as you know, is leading that work. And so we'll partner with the FMA, um, uh, on the development of that question. But I think the thing for me is New Zealanders need access to transaction accounts.

Um, every New Zealander needs that access. It needs to be easy for them, and transaction accounts need to be free and do the things that they should do. And we've launched a new product to achieve just that. And Adam will give you a few details on what that is. Yeah, I think when, when we think about transaction accounts, there's probably sort of two primary dimensions that, that we sort of come at it first, as to Victoria's point is that can we offer a product that is free to essentially do your basic banking services?

So, um, our account, we've got about 390,000 customers on it at the moment. We're projecting that to get to about 600,000 by the middle of the year. And with that account, you can obviously open an account, you can make all of your payments, you can access ATM's, contact center, branch network out digital application, uh, et cetera.

So all of your basic needs can be net met for free. So that's kind of the first dimension. And then the second one is we just wanna make sure that customers don't hold unnecessary balances in those accounts. So we proactively reach out to customers if they've got cer certain balances to see, see whether there's a better product or service that might meet their needs. And, you know, by way of example, we've contacted about 210,000 customers in the last year to, to, for, to see if there's a, a better way for them to be banking,

I think. Yeah, sorry. So when you take that together, I think free transaction accounts for all New Zealanders is really good value. When, when has that, has this, the committee's inquiry, is that what prompted you that, or is this obviously a new initiative you mentioned? So, um, the, the committee did ask questions about what was the term was a basic banking account.

Um, and so that's what we got on and launched a basic banking account. It's a transaction account. And, and as Adam outlined, those are all of the services that come for free. So that was launched in April. April, April. Okay. And just one more supplementary if I could share mm-hmm. When you say proactively reach out, what's the threshold?

Is it certain balance in the account? What's the frequency? How, how are you using that as a metric and, and, um, I guess analyzing that data to continuous improvement? Yeah, there's various different, um, I guess triggers for what might, um, for, for reasons why we might reach out to a customer, a simple one would be if a customer's got more than $500 in their account, we'll reach out and suggest that they open up an savings account.

Similar conversations there for term deposits as well at certain thresholds. So there's various different ways that we will engage. Yeah. And what sort of frequency, Uh, that happens? I mean, we, it's sort of getting that balancing act, right? So we wanna make sure that we're not sort of troubling customers too much with like, contacting them. And so there'll be, 'cause it won't be the only message that we're reaching out to them for.

So it will somewhat depend on sort of holistically how, how often are we talking to them about all of their banking needs. Okay. Okay. Dan, bid. What? Thank you, Mr. Chair. Uh, good to see you again. Uh, Victoria, um, last time you came before the committee, I think, um, it's quite the, uh, the a SB was, um, very strong in it in your thoughts that the RBNZ capital requirements were having an adverse effect on your bank's ability to price lower.

Um, I'm just keen to get a sense from you on what has been announced in December, uh, and what that may mean for, you know, actual reduction in, um, headline interest rates from a SB bank. Yeah. So, um, I think, you know, our points that we were trying to make is that, um, we are very pro harmonization given that capital flows our global harmonization with our other jurisdictions.

And what you can see in the proposals now, um, is that is greater levels of harmonization, if you like, as I would describe it. Um, what it means is that we are still going to have to increase our capital from today. We're still gonna have to hold more levels of capital, but there are some parts of that capital that are going to be cheaper than they otherwise might have been.

Um, net net, uh, we don't see any material cost advantages for that stack, as I call it, that capital stack. We think the biggest impact of the changes is the pro competition element, um, which has been achieved, you know, to support the smaller banks.

Smaller banks. So the, um, lower requirements? That's right. That's specifically, yeah. Okay. So you're saying that actually in terms of from Today? From today, yes. Okay. Yes. Rather than what was gonna be, we are talking about what from today? Yes. I don't think it's gonna be material Compared to today. Okay. Understood. Um, second area is just around the, uh, regulations.

So if sent here in your submission around, um, the Council of Financial Regulators and you're keen to see a greater role of the Ministry of Regulation, um, any areas where you wanna put it on record today that you think the Council of Financial regulators should prioritize? Mm-hmm. Um, 'cause there's many different regulators, there's a lot of overlap.

Yes. Which we've heard here, uh, on countless occasions in the bank's view. What are the areas that they should have as top priority for, um, streamlining or, um, being clear on who's responsible and getting rid of the duplication? Yes. So, um, let's start with duplication When it occurs, we do talk to our regulators.

Um, a simple example, we had, um, uh, one, um, matter, uh, that we were dealing with the primary, a, a primary regulator on, and this is a privacy matter, so the privacy commissioner mm-hmm. Um, we also had three other regulators, um, interested, uh, and all asking different questions in different ways.

And so what we did is we just let them all know and said, Hey, listen, we are dealing with our primary regulator on this matter, and we are happy to provide all that information directly to you. Um, but what we didn't wanna do is talk to four different regulators with different questions and have different information flowing. So the point of that being that when there are, um, overlaps, um, as an example, we try and point that out.

Yes. I think the really big opportunity though is in the UK they, they operate a thing called the regulatory grid. Mm-hmm. And that's something that Australia is taking up, I think it's something New Zealand should take up. And that grid basically, um, looks at all of the regulatory change on foot and tries to create or remove heavy periods, um, of regulatory work.

So I think that's a really, um, helpful tool. Um, and then secondly, what I would say is that I do think that there's this opportunity for regulators to come together and think about the system in its entirety rather than through individual lenses to see if the system as a whole, if we are striking that right balance.

So what I mean, um, I'm very conscious that we've had two decades of the most benign credit impact. Uh, and, you know, we've gotta make sure that we are setting ourselves up for the system of the future and for the environment of the future, not just addressing the past.

Uh, and so I think there's a good opportunity for COVID to really think about all of these changes. Are they setting us up for the future that we're going into? Yeah. Great. Thank you. Okay. I'm gonna go to Dr. Debra Russell. Um, thank you. I'm just very satisfied a SB customer here. Just thank you. Um, uh, and, um, I know Williams, uh, sent her apologies for not being here today.

She had a commitment elsewhere, but she wanted me to ask about, um, asbs bad debt provisions. Yes. Which she says have fallen from, um, I think 17 million to 3 million. It doesn't seem like a lot to me in the first place anyway, given the size of your operation. But that's still quite a sizable, um, decrease in your bad debt debt provisions. What have you done? That's So, um, that number also, by the way, is small relative to the total provisions that we are holding, um, which would be, you know, more sick, the 600 million, so

that's year on year Yeah. Shift. Yeah. Um, but what we are seeing that I think is really positive, um, uh, we have seen, um, an improving situation for our customers. Uh, so a couple of stats that would be top of mind, a 25% decrease in customers who are having difficulty with credit cards, a 20% decrease in customers who are having difficulty with home lending.

Uh, and so, you know, the interest rate environment has definitely been really supportive in that regard. And we've got better processes and systems now to support people earlier, uh, than ever before. And then on the business front, um, we are seeing similar trends. So we are seeing a reduction, um, in businesses and difficulty as well.

So all of that, um, bodes well. Yep. One of the things, um, in that business space that we know that has been happening is that inland revenue has been getting more proactive in chasing after, Uh, Tax debt. Yes. Is that impacting on some of your operations as well, in terms of sustaining some of those? Cut? Um, so we are aware of that dynamic.

Um, and particularly I think some of the lending that came off, um, the COVID period as well from, um, but, uh, obviously when businesses, we have noticed a lot of businesses are choosing to go into receive ship themselves. Uh, and, um, and that obviously impacts, um, all of the accreditors.

So, yeah. So yes. Okay. Thank you. Okay, Nancy, Luke, thank you Chair and welcome, Victoria. Good to see you again. Um, I am interested in the real time payments area, so I know that you, you welcome our recommendation and pushing forward more modernization I do nationally and, and also meeting international, um, I guess behaviors and services that consumers, uh, relate to.

And I particularly note in Asian countries where real time payment, they kind of went from, uh, a savings or check transaction account or checkbook to straight, uh, bypassing all the credit cards stage and went straight to the real time payments. Um, I know that you've answered and said that, you know, you're supporting the Reserve Bank and government agencies setting up that payments roadmap, but for a SB for you Yeah.

And for the work that you do internationally and with your mother, mother bank in Australia, what do you see is the consumer behavior driving there, the, the improvement in the services? And what can you do proactively to recommend back to the Reserve Bank and the government to improve That? Yes. Uh, real time payments.

The, the question is when do you really need them? Mm. When do you need a payment to be absolutely instantaneous? And when is it okay to, um, uh, for it not to necessarily be instantaneous? So one of the developments that the industry has made, um, collectively is there have been requirements, um, for global payments, um, improvements.

So, um, the domestic banks in New Zealand settle seven days a week. We settle on half an hour, um, and we can continue to improve on that half an hour timeframe. So I think there's work that the industry can do, and now that's not real time. The one thing that I think we really, uh, and I've been pretty consistent on this, um, and it's not necessarily a popular view, uh, but we've gotta make sure that we get on top of frauds and scams because real time payments is real time frauds and scams, and it is impossible to get the money back once it's gone, um, in that real time.

So that is the one thing that I wanna be really thoughtful about. Uh, and so I see the work in payments going hand in hand with the work that we're doing, for example, through the New Zealand Antis Scam Alliance, which I think is starting to make real progress on tackling scams, um, as a real collective.

So I'd like to see those two pieces work come together. And what are you studying internationally in other jurisdictions in terms of that working Together? Yeah, so, um, we've done a lot of work looking around the world around how these things are working. Um, one of the trends that we are seeing is, um, a lot more coming together around the concept of economic crime, which is cyber.

Mm-hmm. And, um, frauds and scams and other forms of crime that are developing. I don't think we've yet seen, um, uh, payments and frauds and scams come together in a way that I think we could, because we've got the advantage of being a small country, um, and the advantage of very similar group of, um, participants.

Uh, and so that's what I'd like to see. I think we can do something really good together here if we bring the two together. Okay. Do we have a quick question from Dr. David Wilson? Oh, Well, that's a, uh, sub to what we've been talking about. Um, thank you very much on real-time payments, and you mentioned about frauds and so on. And the fact that the settlement at the moment is like 30 minutes, which, you know, it's a reasonable amount of time.

Um, heard from others though that, um, real-time payments have been around for a long time and other jurisdictions, and subsequently with the rise in birds and scams that, uh, this notion of real time fraud detection is also happening and where people are using algorithms to detect unusual activity.

Yes. And this is already happening, Is we do it all the time. We've been doing it for years. Okay. Okay. Right. I think, we'll, I think we'll leave it at that. So, um, thank you, uh, Victoria. Short, uh, short and, um, Adam Boyd, the chair of a SB, we appreciate that. And can I say that your government relations communications team are bang on and have delivered beautifully on the brief with their very, uh, succinct answers, uh, to recommendations of the, um, to the recommendations that pertain to you.

So thank you for your written submission as well. Great. Thank You. Thank you. Thank you. Okay. Thank you. Right. That brings us to, uh, the a NZ, and we've got, um, Antonio Watson here, chief executive and Scott St. John Chair. So welcome, Mr. St. John and Mrs. Watson. Thank you.

Next Chair. Um, good afternoon everyone, and, and thank you for having us. Um, you've had the chance to read our submission. I had two quick points that I wanted to make before we started, but Victoria made both of them exactly the way I would've made them. One was around the Reserve Bank's capital Review. Um, it didn't go as far as the committee recommended, which was to stop at now, but it has improved.

And I think her, um, point about the pro competitiveness of it has been a particular feature of it. And the second one is competition. We obviously welcome competition. It makes us better. We absolutely support the committee's work in that space. And I was gonna use December as an example as well, of some of the absolutely fierce competition we've seen lately.

And certainly if you talk to anyone on our front lines, they'll tell you a bit. And competition in our case isn't just price, it's price, it's approval process, it's incentives that we're being offered, it's turnaround times, all those kind of things. So, um, happy to answer any questions. Okay. Um, I, I'll kick it off with, um, we've, we had, um, a FinTech entrepreneur present last week.

And in his submission, he's, uh, respectfully requested that this committee committee consider holding the banks and financial services industry to account for how they engage and support local fintechs without genuine engagement and support from the incumbent banks. Uh, those local FinTech companies risk failing because they can't, uh, before they can scale, um, to, to, to an adequate amount.

And so I, I put it to you, what's the a NZ as the biggest show in town, if you like. What's your response to concerns around from fintechs around support and engagement And, and that it's difficult when you're a small startup business? And I absolutely acknowledge that, um, the regulatory sandbox that has been created, and I know you heard a little bit about that, is a help in that space.

'cause that allows a proof, sort of a proof of concept is what you are doing. Correct. But we actually welcome, we are, we are net deep in modernizing our banking platforms. We've got old legacy platforms, if I'm honest, you know, going back a couple of years, fintechs would approach us with a great idea. We'd feel slightly sick because we would know how hard it would be to plum in with the great idea.

Um, open banking and the, and the API standards help with that. But, um, my point being that actually working with fin, you know, these are people that have great ideas that stop us needing to do something. So we are very, very willing to work with them. We have engaged with all the fintechs that belong to the A API center at the moment.

We are not directly investing in them at this stage. I know some of our peers are, and that's, there's different opinions about whether that's a good idea or not. Um, but in terms of being welcoming innovation, that means that we are not having to do it in house ourselves. It's just a matter of being able to really, um, well really easily plug them in.

And the API stands have helped a lot with that. So I think we'll see a bit more of a, a bell wave of that coming through now. Okay. Uh, Ryan Hil, Thanks and thanks again for your submission. And, and again, that was probably quite a wholesome one, so I appreciate the work you put into these. Um, I guess two questions. One, um, just in terms of a candid response from you, obviously the banking inquiry solicit a bit of media and a bit of attention, but what do you think has been the significant shift, if any, in a Z's culture in terms of, you know,

just upping the ante and appreciate you already acknowledge your customer focused. And my second one I'll come to is around payments New Zealand. Okay. So, um, upping the ante. So absolutely, you know, um, the market study and then followed by this inquiry and, you know, we welcome the opportunity to continue to engage it. It shines a light in the industry and I think that's helpful.

It holds us to account, um, competition makes us better. Making sure that the environment is welcome to competition will make us better. And as you'll have seen in our submission, there are a number of the, um, recommendations that we've already implemented or are partway through printing and that will be good for our customers. And Thank you. And Payments New Zealand, obviously this came up through the earlier committee, you know, sessions around the, um, the shareholding.

And, and you guys obviously are a significant part of that as a NZ. Um, how do you think that's progressing? You, you get a bit of criticism around, you know, it's, um, yeah, Turkey's voting for Christmas a little bit, but, um, there is a lot of work coming out of it. There is some pressure from, um, FMA, um, reserve Bank, you know, all these different entities are wanting to move towards, we're hearing a lot about real time payments, et cetera.

But then it's also the spectrum of risk. How do you think that space is moving? Um, do you think Payments New Zealand is a bottleneck? Do you think other people are bottlenecks or do you think the pace is actually okay? I think the pace, the payment systems are really difficult and complex and there aren't many experts in them.

I think that, um, some of the commentary has been around, you know, as you say, Turkey's voted Christmas. We kind of had to create Christmas 'cause no one else has done it for us. So, for example, payments, New Zealand owns the API center that's running open banking. No one else was saying, oh, well let's put that in a regulated space, or let's make it a government entity. So it's sort of, we've done it to ourselves because we needed to do it to ourselves.

And Payments New Zealand was set up a long time ago to sort of manage the payment rails of New Zealand. They, you know, we have to respect their independence. We do have a seat on the board, but equally there are independent directors. Um, we know that they're actively addressing some of the, um, whether it's perceptional reality about, about their structure, um, that actively working on the future of payments.

Now, we would hope that that would be an aligned conversation with regulators. 'cause we know that the Reserve Bank is actively working on the same thing. And I guess another question for you, uh, we've got, um, Adrian from Blink Pay. There was an article came out I think yesterday saying he actually feels we can make big advances on open banking under the current rails.

Um, whereas others might say, let's look at India, who, who built a completely new, um, uh, payment system New Zealand. What's your view? Have we got enough? Can we, can we maximize the current infrastructure? Or, and or do we need to continue investing in the new? I I very much like the idea of being able to maximize the current infrastructure and develop on it.

Because India was started in a very different position. They started in a position where money was changing hands in cash and markets, you know, the, the money lender was charging at some egregious interest rate for borrowing money. And what they've done is phenomenal, but we are not in their starting position. Mm-hmm. So I think, you know, Adrian's ideas about whether we can use even the open banking rails to help with real time payments in certain circumstances, it wouldn't be all circumstances, but back to some of the points that have been made,

you need to look at the use cases. I mean, I'm very happy and I think most merchants are very happy that I can pay instantly. They know that my, they, they know I'm good for the money, so we have to look at the use cases as well. So I think that something very, um, very deliberate and focused on where we are now, where we'd like to get to, and the easiest way to get there.

Because every dollar we spend on payments, we're not spending on some other form of innovation. So we just need to be careful that we balance across the industry where we want the innovation to appear. Thank You. Okay. Uh, Dan Bidar. Thank you Chair. Uh, good to see you both again. Um, in the previous submission you made a case that the old capital ratio requirements were going to have a quantum impact on your, uh, level of interest rates in the order of 45 to 75 basis points.

Uh, and you're saying you suggested that at the point of Vem, which is a few months ago, um, the, the current cost was about 25 basis points in light of what the Reserve Bank announced last year, uh, admitting that it is not as far as you wish it to go. Um, has the a NZ as the biggest bank, the mother of all bank in New Zealand, um, uh, done any analysis on the cost savings as a basis point from the Reserve Bank capital requirement changes?

Yeah, so, so the Reserve Bank said when they, when they announced the changes that they felt that that would be, I think it was seven to 15 points less upward pressure on interest rates. So don't forget, this is a, we're talking about the future. We are where we are. Um, we see that as, as, um, on average as a six basis points, less upward pressure on interest rates, um, that will manifest differently over our portfolio.

The more risky lending that will be more of a, I'm not gonna say a saving 'cause it isn't necessarily a drop from now. It's a less, less of that upward pressure because we are not having to put as much of the more expensive types of capital in particular into the, into our capital stack. Okay. So still an increase, but six basis Points, but less, but less. So.

Thank you. Okay. Any other questions, members? No. Um, the, uh, Prudential Policy Committee, the financial policy committee that the Reserve Bank announced, uh, its charter has been rolled out and its, uh, um, uh, members are being, uh, organized and the first meeting is, uh, imminent. Um, what do you think should be its priorities, uh, for the financial policy committee, the nons statutory committee that the Reserve Bank have set up?

I think that goes back to some of the conversation already, which is around, um, we, we strongly believe that the more we can be, um, consistent with other like jurisdictions, for example, the more that we can be consistent, um, across regulator and make sure that each regulator's playing, you know, playing a particular role.

I think it's around those sorts of things. Just making sure we are very deliberate about where regulation is really important because, or, you know, it is really important that we have a safe, stable, trusted banking, um, banking, um, system. But there is a balance on, you know, the cost of borrowing or the friction we have in our payment systems or, or lots of different things that need to be balanced out.

So I think it would be, you know, a really sensible, where are we going to land on that spectrum of dead safe banks to, you know, incredibly innovative banks, but maybe we might lose some trust in 'em in the community. So we, we are on that spectrum, do we want to be as a country? Because that's not a decision that banks can make.

That's a decision for government and regulators, I think. Mm-hmm. Um, in November, you posted a, uh, $2.53 billion profit for the A NZ. Um, how do you think 2026 or your next financial year is, is tracking? Um, so that, that I can't give you the forward looking information.

Obviously that's, um, that's, um, sensitive, market sensitive information. But what we're seeing this year is we had an, um, a one-off in that, in that number, so that we have, um, some hedging that sort of fluctuates around. So when you peel that back, our profit was lower. Um, we would ex we wouldn't expect it to be significantly higher, significantly lower.

Um, things are the, when you look at the, the economic environment, we are very well provided for bad debts, which tends to be where you see big bank losses. Our margins are stable to downward, downward pressure on them because of the interest rate environment at the moment. So that's kind of where we'd be looking at similar, you know, slight, you know, somewhere modestly above our cost of capital returns is really what we're looking for. You Get a modicum of market growth.

Yeah. And there's obviously market growth every year. Mm-hmm. First home buyer, um, market, that category is, uh, seems to be stronger than ever in the, in a generation. Is that certainly reflecting in the business and lending that you are doing? Yes, absolutely. We're really seeing first time buy, first time buyers come to the market. Part of that I think is house price.

You know, interest rates have come down, house prices have stabilized, um, so there's a bit more certainty there. Um, and incomes have grown over the last few years as well. So that's been really pleasing to see. Okay. Any other questions or observations members? No. No. Okay. Thank you so much. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you for your written submission and, uh, appearing before us today.

Thank you. Okay. That brings us to the com column affectionately called the ComCom. Um, and we've got, uh, Dr. John Small here as Chair and colleague and and me head of market studies. Ah, good afternoon to you both. Thank you for your patience.

We, we are running author of the report that there's not one author to that sort of thing that of the project. Yeah. There's many fa there's many fathers or mothers to success, isn't it? Absolute. Okay. Um, right. We look forward to your introductory comments. We've got your supplementary, uh, uh, submissions here, and, uh, then we'll open up for questions.

Yeah. Thanks very much for the, for the, for the time. Um, uh, so as you know, the comm Commission's job really is promoting competition. Um, and, uh, following on from the banking study, we've been doing quite a bit of follow up work to, um, check and, and on, uh, the recommendations that we've made, um, that's been the focus of the report that we've, we've given to you.

Um, a lot of that is on track. Um, probably the main sort of multilateral, um, issue that's outstanding is this, uh, technology that we think could be, that we've seen working in Australia that we haven't got in New Zealand that would, um, speed up and allow mortgage, um, uh, advisors to, uh, get multiple quotes from banks in a hurry.

So that piece is, is a little bit stuck. And so anything the committee could do to push that on, I mean, it's easy to see the reasons why that's stuck. Um, many of the other recommendations are, are, are reasonably well on track. Um, I think probably the only other comment to make at a high level is that there's been a lot of talk about open banking and, um, we think, I guess we see open banking as a means to an end, uh, rather than end in itself.

It is important that we make it work properly. Um, but certainly at the commission, the reason that we thought it was a great thing was because we, we really want to see actual moderate scale disruption for the large banks that you've just been listening to, and that's yet to happen.

Mm-hmm. Uh, so that, that's, I'll just leave it there. Okay. We're gonna go to Dr. Deborah SEL first. Um, thank you. Um, I think we've been hearing from the large banks, and there was about a whole lot of switching going on in December, I presume November, December last year, as rates sort of bottomed out and then started to look like they might rise again.

Have you got, did you, did you see that as an increased level of competition too? We are getting sort of just slightly conflicting views on how much switching there really was between your numbers. And I I think we're probably working off the same data so we can all see the data. Yeah. Um, I, I mean, I had hoped that that was partly attributable to, um, the new switching service that payments New Zealand put in and have been promoting.

Um, I think it's, it's healthy, um, but people are shopping around a little more. So that's a, I'd say that's a good sign. Um, it's also probably a, you know, a product of, uh, the broader economy.
